coredata.dc:title: Theoretical study of hydrogen adsorption on the graphene quantum dots doped with various first row transition metals: Switch of spin state as a way to improve H2 adsorption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Malček, Michal
Müllerová, Simona
Bučinský, Lukáš
coredata.dc:description: A rapid increase of human population in the last years stimulates a search for alternative renewable and environmentally friendly energy resources. Hydrogen energy becomes one of the most promising candidates to replace the largely consumed fossil fuels. The first crucial step in the process of obtaining the hydrogen energy is an efficient storage of hydrogen gas. Modified graphene nanomaterials have already proved their capability as adsorbents of gas molecules. An affinity to bind, and subsequently store, hydrogen molecules is investigated using density functional theory (DFT) for the series of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped with different transition metals. Overall, considering the calculated binding energies and bonding distances, the Fe-doped GQD is the most promising material for H2 capture, with storage capacity limited to three H2 molecules on one Fe atom. Further potential candidates for effective H2 adsorption are Mn-, and Cr-doped GQDs. Our calculations suggest that an induced change of their spin states may significantly enhance their H2 adsorption ability. Such a change of spin state would be more easily accomplished in the case of Mn-doped GQD, having the doublet and quartet spin state energetically closer to each other (ca. 16.5 kJ mol−1).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coronene
DFT
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrogen storage
Transition metals

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TM-doped coronene and circumcoronene: basis set performance and spin state preference
Hydrogen binding performance of C-TM and CC-TM: binding energies, bonding distances, and a comparison to N2 affinity
Hydrogen storage capacity of the CC-Fe and CC-Mn

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** coronene (C, left) and circumcoronene (CC, right).

Scheme of B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** optimized circumcoronene structures with different transition metals (CC-TM). Colors of atoms: C – grey, H – white, Cr – orange, Mn – purple, Fe – red, Co – wine, Ni – cyan, Zn – dark grey.

Scheme of B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** optimized TM-doped circumcoronene plus interacting H2 molecule (CC-TM + H2). Colors of atoms: C – grey, H – white, Cr – orange, Mn – purple, Fe – red, Co – wine, Ni – cyan, Zn – dark grey.
Scheme of the side-on (left) and end-on (right) attack of the hydrogen molecule on the TM dopant atom (in this case Fe). Colors of atoms: C – grey, H – white, Fe – red.
Scheme of B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** optimized doublet Mn-doped (left) and Fe-doped (right) circumcoronene plus interacting H2 molecules. Colors of atoms: C – grey, H – white, Mn – purple, Fe – red.

PDOS plots of Mn–H and Fe–H orbitals in CC-Mn+3H2 (left) and CC-Fe+3H2 (right), respectively. All plots are obtained at the B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** level of theory and the absolute scale is used for comparison purposes.

B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** total SCF and relative energies ΔE (in a.u. or kJ.mol−1) in the studied systems with different multiplicity (M S). The most stable spin state of the particular systems is highlighted in bold.
Calculated B3LYP-GD3 adsorption energies ΔE ads of the transition metal (TM)-doped coronene and circumcoronene employing selected basis sets (M S = spin multiplicity).
Calculated B3LYP-GD3 TM-H distances of the transition metal (TM)-doped coronene and circumcoronene employing selected basis sets (M S = spin multiplicity).
Calculated B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** TM-H distances of the doublet CC-Mn + N H2 and CC-Fe + N H2 (#N = number of H2 molecules).

head.ce:title: Theoretical study of hydrogen adsorption on the graphene quantum dots doped with various first row transition metals: Switch of spin state as a way to improve H2 adsorption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physical Chemistry and Chemical Physics, Faculty of Chemical and Food Technology, Slovak University of Technology in Bratislava, Radlinského 9, 812 37, Bratislava, Slovak Republic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Physical Chemistry and Chemical Physics
Faculty of Chemical and Food Technology
Slovak University of Technology in Bratislava

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Physical Chemistry and Chemical Physics, Faculty of Chemical and Food Technology, Slovak University of Technology in Bratislava, Radlinskeho 9, 812 37, Bratislava, Slovak Republic

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid increase of human population in the last years stimulates a search for alternative renewable and environmentally friendly energy resources. Hydrogen energy becomes one of the most promising candidates to replace the largely consumed fossil fuels. The first crucial step in the process of obtaining the hydrogen energy is an efficient storage of hydrogen gas. Modified graphene nanomaterials have already proved their capability as adsorbents of gas molecules. An affinity to bind, and subsequently store, hydrogen molecules is investigated using density functional theory (DFT) for the series of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped with different transition metals. Overall, considering the calculated binding energies and bonding distances, the Fe-doped GQD is the most promising material for H2 capture, with storage capacity limited to three H2 molecules on one Fe atom. Further potential candidates for effective H2 adsorption are Mn-, and Cr-doped GQDs. Our calculations suggest that an induced change of their spin states may significantly enhance their H2 adsorption ability. Such a change of spin state would be more easily accomplished in the case of Mn-doped GQD, having the doublet and quartet spin state energetically closer to each other (ca. 16.5 kJ mol−1).

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decades, the growing human population puts extreme demands on Earth's energy sources and environment. Hence, the search for clean and renewable energy resources to replace the largely consumed fossil fuels is of urge importance. Hydrogen gas becomes one of the most promising of such resources due to its excellent energy carrier properties and renewability [1]. To obtain a clean and so-called “green” hydrogen energy, the attention turns to environmentally friendly hydrogen production [2–4] as well as to its efficient storage [1,5]. Hydrogen gas (H2) storage under ambient conditions can be difficult due to the weak interactions among the H2 molecules. The first step in every storage process (in this case H2 storage) is the adsorption on the given material. An effective adsorption of the host molecule is driven by the sensitivity of the particular adsorbent towards binding of the targeted species. Different metal oxides or two-dimensional materials are one of the most common gas sensors and adsorbents [5,6]. This idea to use two-dimensional graphene materials as adsorbents of individual gas molecules has been proposed by Schedin et al. [7] in 2007. Since its rediscovery in 2004 [8], graphene based materials have attracted a huge attention due to their extraordinary chemical, mechanical, electronic and optical properties [7–16]. However, the pristine graphene behaves like a zero-band-gap semiconductor [17], hence the hydrogen molecule exhibited only weak affinity towards the pristine graphene surface (GS) [18–20]. It has been shown that doping (replacing a carbon atom with the dopant atom) or decorating (placing a heteroatom above the carbon layer) of the GS with different heteroatoms opens its band-gap and subsequently improves the electronic properties and chemical sensitivity of GS [21–30]. Consequently, a number of computational studies of H2 storage performance of differently modified graphene surfaces is increasing [18,20,31–37]. It has been reported that the storage ability of the doped GS is three H2 molecules on one transition metal (e.g. Cr [18], Os [20], Li [31], Cu [36]. On the other hand, in the case of the decorated GS, the number of adsorbed H2 molecules is higher, in particular four H2 molecules on one Pd atom [35] or even five H2 molecules on one Cu atom [36]. Besides the graphene based nanomaterials, modified borophene [32,38] or black phosphorene [39] can be used as H2 storage materials as well.

In one of our previous works, we have reported that the inclusion of diffuse functions (i.e. 6-311G* vs. 6–311++G** basis set used) affects the interactions between Cu dopant atom and circumcoronene backbone [36]. In the recent years, different research groups have reported theoretical studies of doped or decorated graphene surfaces using different basis sets, for example 6-31G* [40,41], 6-31+G* [28,37,42], 6-311G** [19,43], 6–311++G** [36], LanL2DZ [20,22,29,44–46], TZVPP [47], Def2TZVP [39], or Vanderbilt pseudopotentials [31]. It is obvious that the increasing complexity of the basis set used goes hand in hand with an increase of the computational cost, especially in the case of large systems such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [9,14]. GQDs are layered structures derived from graphene with a size up to 100 nm [48]. In contrast, carbon quantum dots are small carbon nanoparticles possessing spherical shape with a diameter up to 10 nm [48].

In this respect, the first goal of the current study is to examine the performance of different basis sets to provide orbital populations for GQDs doped with selected first row transition metal (TM) atoms, in particular Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Zn.

In addition, the previous QTAIM analysis of bond critical points showed that the interaction between the Cu and adsorbed H2 molecules in the studied systems is weak and can be considered as a physisorption [36]. Such interaction may not be strong enough for efficient H2 adsorption and its subsequent storage. Cu affinity towards H2 can be increased for example by the presence of nitrogen atoms in the vicinity of Cu in Cu-decorated N-doped GS as reported recently by Singla and Jaggi [37]. Still, there exist differently modified GSs, e.g. Cr-doped [18], Fe-doped or Os-doped [20] ones, which seem to exhibit a better H2 storage performance when compared to the Cu-doped ones.

The second task of this study is to compare the H2 adsorption ability of the different TM-doped GQDs. In this place it has to be mentioned that the studied TM dopants can have different spin state multiplicities (M S) with respect to their valence electron configuration. Even though the multiplicity of the particular TM may play a role in its interatomic interactions [49–53], the spin state preference of the Cr, Fe, Co or Ni dopants in GQDs has often not been discussed [18,20,29].

Coronene (C) and circumcoronene (CC) are polyaromatic hydrocarbons suitable to be used as model systems of tiny graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [18–20,36,43–46,54]. C and CC are composed of 24 or 54 carbon atoms arranged in 7 or 19 hexagonal rings, respectively. Optimized geometries of these two highly symmetric molecules (B3LYP/6-311G* level of theory) were taken from authors' previous works [19,36,43]. These geometries were re-optimized using the B3LYP [55–58] functional including the D3 version of Grimme's dispersion correction (GD3) [59] and 6-311G** basis set [60,61]. B3LYP-GD3 approach is a suitable method to treat weak intermolecular interactions in the vicinity of graphene sheets when taking into account its reasonable accuracy and relatively low computational cost [62–64]. Structure of the optimized coronene (C) and circumcoronene (CC) is shown in Fig. 1 . Both studied systems (C and CC) were subsequently doped with one transition metal (TM) atom by replacing one carbon within their backbone. Note that, in the case of CC only the most stable dopant position is considered [19]. The set of first-row transition metals used as dopants consists of Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Zn (note that, Cu is omitted because it has been previously studied in our group [19,36,43]).

In the next step, the interacting H2 molecule (or molecules) was placed in the vicinity of the TM dopant atom in all the studied systems with the initial distance between the H2 and TM being from the interval of 2.2–2.4 Å. Geometry optimizations of all doped systems (alone as well as including H2 molecule) were performed at the B3LYP-GD3 [55–59] level of theory employing different basis sets: 6-311G**, 6–311++G** [60,61,65], Def2TZV, Def2TZVP, Def2TZVPP [66], LanL2DZ [67,68], and a mixed basis set of LanL2DZ for metal atoms and 6-31G** for non-metal atoms. For the sake of comparison, additional geometry optimizations of all doped coronenes were performed at the BLYP-GD3 [55,56,59]/6-311G** level of theory. All of the aforementioned calculations were carried out using the Gaussian16 program package [69] and the default settings of the calculation parameters were employed, if not stated otherwise. The SCF convergence was set to tight [70]. QTAIM analysis [71] as implemented in the AIMAll package [72] was performed using the B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** Gaussian16 checkpoint files. This analysis was employed to estimate the character of interatomic (or intermolecular) interactions via the bond critical points (BCP) descriptors and the delocalization indexes (DI) values. Density of states (DOS) analysis was carried out using the Gauss-Sum package [73] to get insight into the molecular orbitals overlapping. Visualization of the calculated structures was performed using the Molekel software suite [74].

The basis set superposition error (BSSE) corrected ΔE ads adsorption energies between the TM-doped GQDs and the H2 molecule were calculated using the equation:
(1)
Δ
E
a d s
=
E
T M − G Q D / H 2
−
E
T M − G Q D
+
E
H 2
where E TM-GQD/H2 is the energy of system with adsorbed molecules, E TM-GQD and E H2 are the BSSE corrected energies of TM-doped GQDs and the H2 molecule, respectively, via the counterpoise scheme of Boys and Bernardi [75]. Quadratically convergent (QC) SCF procedure [76] was employed because of the problematic convergence of some of the counterpoise calculations (mainly in the case of systems containing Cr or Mn).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TM-doped coronene and circumcoronene: basis set performance and spin state preference
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** TM-doped circumcoronenes (CC-TM) are shown in Fig. 2 . In addition, the optimized (B3LYP-GD3/6-311G**) structures of the TM-doped coronenes (C-TM) are presented in Fig. S1. Presence of the TM dopant leads to perturbations in the originally planar carbon honeycomb structure of the GQDs, due to the larger number of the electron shells in the particular TM when compared to the replaced carbon atom. Such perturbations are well documented in the literature, see e.g. Refs. [19–22,29,31]. As can be seen from Fig. 2 and Fig. S1, these perturbations are the most pronounced in the cases of Zn-doped systems, what is expected because Zn contains the highest number of electrons among the studied TM dopant atoms.

Mulliken Gross orbital populations of valence electrons are presented in Fig. S2. As can be clearly seen from Fig. S2, the inclusion of diffuse functions (i.e. using 6–311++G** basis set) leads to overestimated s and p valence populations in the case of Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped GQDs, see green, olive, red and magenta triangles in Fig. S2(a–c). In addition, similar shift can be found in p valence populations of Co-, Ni-, and Zn-doped systems as well, see green and olive triangles in Fig. S2(d–f). On the other hand, calculated d populations are in qualitative agreement for all the basis sets used, see blue and cyan marks in Fig. S2. Differences between the results obtained by using 6-311G** and 6–311++G** basis sets reported herein are in agreement with our previously published data [36]. The obtained results can be assigned to the sensitivity of the Mulliken population analysis to the presence of diffuse functions in the 6–311++G** basis set. Another interesting point is the performance of relatively small and computationally “cheap” LanL2DZ basis set. This frequently used basis set (see e.g. Refs. [20,22,29,44–46]) provides orbital populations comparable with the ones obtained by the significantly larger and computationally more “expensive” basis sets (Def2TZV, Def2TZVP, Def2TZVPP). In addition, similar results were obtained also for the mixed basis set (LanL2DZ for metals and 6-31G** for non-metal atoms). Last but not least, the calculated orbital populations of C-TM and CC-TM have, in general, similar trends. Hence, one can conclude that C-TM, being the smaller and computationally cheaper system when compared to CC-TM, represents a suitable model for the calculations of the dopant atom properties, like e.g. adsorption energies [15]. However, in the case of calculations of the material properties at the nanoscale level, larger systems like e. g CC-TM [22,43,44], carbon nanocones [41] or periodic graphene [9,77] should be employed.

Further and crucial issue which has to be considered is the spin multiplicity (M S) of the particular TM dopants, both before and after H2 adsorption. Total as well as relative SCF energies of the studied systems with different multiplicities (with respect to the particular number of valence electrons) are shown in Table 1 . As can be seen from Table 1, in the case of Co- and Ni-doped GQDs the lowest spin state (singlet or doublet, respectively) is the energetically preferred one. Similarly, Ghambarian et al. have also reported that the singlet state of Ni dopant is energetically more preferred than triplet [39]. The low-spin state (singlet) is preferred also in the case of Zn-doped systems, except the Zn-doped coronene (C–Zn), where the triplet state is energetically below the singlet. Still, the energy difference between these two states is low (ca. 6 kJ mol−1, corresponding to Boltzmann population ratio of approximately 8%:92% for singlet:triplet at 298.15 K). On the other hand, in the case of Cr- and Mn-doped systems, spin states with higher multiplicities are the energetically preferred ones, in particular, triplet and quartet for Cr and Mn, respectively. Interestingly, in the case of Fe-doped systems, the adsorption of H2 molecule results in the change of the energy preference from the triplet (CC–Fe) to a singlet spin state (CC–Fe + H2), see Table 1. Similar change from the triplet to singlet spin state preference upon H2 adsorption have been reported also for Fe containing organometallic complexes [78]. Such a switch of spin state preference from high-spin to low-spin upon H2 adsorption is possible because the singlet and triplet states of the C–Fe and CC-Fe are also energetically close to each other (ΔE values less than 10 kJ mol−1). Moreover, the H2 is a strong ligand field moiety, hence its presence favors the existence of transition metal complexes in their low-spin states [79].

For the sake of comparison, total and relative SCF energies of the C-TM systems before and after H2 adsorption calculated at the BLYP-GD3 and B3LYP levels of theory are presented in Table S1. The energetically preferred spin states of the systems after H2 adsorption presented therein (see Table S1) are in accordance with the ones obtained at the B3LYP-GD3 level of theory (see Table 1). The only exception is C–Mn + H2, where BLYP-GD3 method favors the doublet spin state while B3LYP-GD3 method favors the quartet spin state. Such an observation is in accordance with the fact that the high-spin state is often more favored in the hybrid DFT (B3LYP) calculations when compared to pure GGA (BLYP) ones [80,81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen binding performance of C-TM and CC-TM: binding energies, bonding distances, and a comparison to N2 affinity
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** CC-TM systems with adsorbed H2 molecule (in their energetically most preferred spin states) are shown in Fig. 3 . For completeness, optimized (B3LYP-GD3/6-311G**) structures of the smaller C-TM systems with adsorbed H2 molecule are presented in Fig. S3.

For the sake of simplicity only four basis sets, in particular 6-311G**, 6–311++G**, Def2TZVP and LanL2DZ, are considered from hereafter. Basis sets Def2TZV and Def2TZVPP were omitted due to the similar performance when compared to Def2TZVP. In the case of Cr- and Mn-doped systems two different spin states (the lowest one and the most preferred one) are considered, what will be discussed later. As can be seen from the calculated adsorption energies (Table 2 ) and the calculated TM-H2 distances (Table 3 ), the strongest ability to bind H2 molecule is found in the case of the singlet state of the Cr-doped systems having ΔE ads values within the range of −55 to −86 kJ mol−1 with respect to the basis set used. On the other hand, the H2 adsorption ability is significantly decreased for the energetically more stable triplet state Cr-doped systems (ΔE ads values from the interval of −14 to −23 kJ mol−1). For comparison, an enhanced H2 adsorption over Cr-doped GQDs has been already reported by Xiang and coworkers [18]. The calculated PBE adsorption energy of the first H2 molecule on Cr-doped GQD reported by Xiang et al. is −0.574 eV (i.e. −55.4 kJ mol−1). This value is comparable with our LanL2DZ result obtained for the singlet state Cr-doped systems (−55.4 or −66.9 kJ mol−1, see Table 2). Unfortunately, Xiang and coworkers [18] do not mention the spin state of Cr atom in their calculations. The situation is the same in the case of Mn-doped GQDs: while the doublet spin state Mn-doped systems are prone to H2 binding (ΔE ads values up to −58 kJ mol−1), their energetically preferred quartet state analogs show rather a limited H2 adsorption performance (ΔE ads values less negative than −14 kJ mol−1). Hence, one can conclude that the Cr or Mn dopant atoms can be used as suitable H2 adsorption sites but not in their most stable spin states. With this respect, an induced spin state switch in Cr- and Mn-doped GQDs may present a way to improve their ability to bind and adsorb the H2 molecule. Such a switch would be accomplished more easily in the case of Mn-doped GQDs, where the energy difference between the doublet and quartet state after H2 adsorption is around 16.5 kJ mol−1. For comparison, the energy difference between the singlet and triplet state of Cr-doped GQDs after H2 adsorption is larger than 90 kJ mol−1. Induced spin state transitions (spin-crossover) have been recently reported for e.g. the Co–N-doped graphene using an applied external potential [82] or Fe–N-doped graphene nanoribbon using a mechanically applied strain [83].

Besides Cr and Mn, another promising system for H2 adsorption is the singlet state Fe-doped GQD, with the found ΔE ads values from the interval of −34 to −58 kJ mol−1. Moreover, singlet is the energetically most stable spin state of Fe-doped systems after H2 adsorption, see Table 1. This combination of findings indicates that, among the studied systems, the Fe-doped GQDs are overall most suitable H2 adsorption materials, when considering the binding energies (Table 2) and TM-H bonding distances (Table 3). On the other hand, the calculated H2 adsorption energies are significantly less negative in the case of Co-, Ni-, and Zn-doped systems (ΔE ads values are within the range of −8 to −22 kJ mol−1). According to Jeffrey [84], the energy of a moderate hydrogen bond is from the interval of 16–63 kJ mol−1. The bottom of this interval (around 16 kJ mol−1) corresponds to the ΔE ads values of Co-, Ni-, and Zn-doped systems, while the top of this interval (around 63 kJ mol−1) is achieved by the H2 binding energies of Fe-, singlet Cr-, and doublet Mn-doped systems, see Table 2. Finally, as for the comparison of the basis sets used: LanL2DZ basis set provides slightly longer TM-H2 distances and consequently less negative adsorption energies when compared to other basis sets (6-311G**, 6–311++G** and Def2TZVP).

Calculated TM-H2 distances (Table 3) are in accordance with the calculated adsorption energies (Table 2), i.e. shorter TM-H distance means a more negative ΔE ads value. All of the presented TM-doped GQDs bind H2 molecule in a side-on way, which is documented by the two almost identical TM-H distances in every single system under study (differences are less than 0.1 Å, see Table 3). The side-on H2 binding is also called η2-dihydrogen bond and it leads to an elongation of the H–H distance from 0.74 Å to 0.8–0.9 Å [85,86]. For clarity, schematic drawings of side-on and end-on attack of the hydrogen molecule on the dopant atom are shown in Fig. 4 . All of the calculated TM-H distances for singlet Cr-, doublet Mn-, and Fe-doped systems are lower than 2 Å, indicating “chemical” interaction, while TM-H2 distances in the case of triplet Cr-, quartet Mn-, Co-, Ni-, and Zn-doped systems are from the interval of 2.2–2.8 Å, pointing out to rather “physical” (or van der Waals) mode of binding. Considering all the distances presented in Table 3, one finds that LanL2DZ basis set provides slightly longer TM-H2 distances (and consequently less negative adsorption energies) when compared to other basis sets (6-331G**, 6–311++G** and Def2TZVP). The only exception is the Ni-doped coronene. For comparison, our calculated LanL2DZ Fe–H2 and Co–H2 distances are in agreement with the ones obtained by Tabtimsai et al. [20] (B3LYP/LanL2DZ). Interestingly, in the case of Ni-doped GQDs our LanL2DZ Ni–H2 distances (being ca. 2.5 Å) do not correspond well with the ones of Tabtimsai et al. (being 2.1 Å) [20], while, the 6-311G** or Def2TZVP Ni–H2 distances (being ca. 2.0 Å) are again in good agreement with the LanL2DZ ones of Tabtimsai et al. (2.1 Å) [20]. In addition, Ni used as a dopant atom on black phosphorene has been reported as a suitable H2 adsorption site by Ghambarian et al. [39]. The calculated Ni–H2 distance (PBE/Def2SVP) in the work of Ghambarian et al. is 1.76 Å, which is significantly shorter than the Pd–H2 or Pt–H2 distances (around 2.6 Å) in Pd-, and Pt-doped black phosphorenes reported in the same work [39].

For the sake of comparison, calculated 6-311G** B3LYP-GD3, BLYP-GD3 and B3LYP TM-H distances of the TM-doped coronenes are presented in Table S2. Even though the inclusion of GD3 correction in some of the studied cases leads to longer TM-H distances, this inclusion is essential for a proper treatment of adsorption energies [36,62–64] (especially for those cases where the B3LYP-GD3 ΔE ads values are lower than −15 kJ. mol−1). For example, calculated 6-311G** ΔE ads values of the C–Mn + H2 (C–Co + H2) at the B3LYP-GD3, BLYP-GD3 and B3LYP are −11.8, −10.4 and + 1.0 kJ mol−1 (−9.2, −8.6 and + 6.2 kJ mol−1), respectively.

Further information about the nature of the TM-H2 interaction can be obtained from the Bader's QTAIM analysis of bond critical points (BCPs) and delocalization indexes [71]. Calculated B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** charge densities in BCPs (ρ BCP), their Laplacians (Δρ BCP) and corresponding delocalization indexes (DI) of the studied systems are summarized in Table S3. Naturally, QTAIM data presented in Table S3 are in accord with the calculated TM-H2 bond distances shown in Table 3. DI values of around 0.3 found for the singlet Cr-, doublet Mn-, and Fe-doped GQDs indicate η2-dihydrogen character of the TM-H2 bond, while the lower DI values (around 0.1) obtained in the case of triplet Cr-, quartet Mn-, Co-, Ni-, and Zn-doped systems point on the physisorption of the H2 molecule. Note that, according to Bader and Stephens, the DI parameter is a measure of the number of electrons shared between the two atoms [87].

Another issue which should be examined when studying the ability to bind hydrogen gas is a presence of an interfering medium. Nitrogen (N2), due to its high natural abundance in air as well as its inertness is a suitable choice [39]. In this respect we have optimized all the studied TM-doped coronenes including an interacting N2 molecule. Scheme of the optimized (B3LYP-GD3/6-311G**) CC-TM + N2 structures is presented in Fig. S4, and a comparison between the TM-N2 and the corresponding TM-H2 distances is shown in Table S4. As can be seen from Table S4, in the case of singlet Cr-, doublet Mn-, and Fe-doped coronenes, the calculated TM-N distances are longer than the TM-H ones about approximately 0.3 Å. These differences in distances indicate the stronger affinity of the particular TM towards the H2 molecule when compared to the N2, what is an important factor for successful H2 adsorption under inert (nitrogen) atmosphere. On the contrary, triplet Cr-, quartet Mn-, Co-, Ni-, and Zn-doped coronenes interact with N2 molecule in an end-on way where only the proximal N of the N2 molecule binds to the TM dopant (TM-N distance is around 2 Å). Such an interaction is significantly stronger than the one with H2 molecule (TM-H distances are around 2.5 Å, see Table S4), hence, from this point of view, these systems are not as suitable H2 adsorbents as the singlet Cr-, doublet Mn-, and Fe-doped GQDs, what is in accordance with the results presented above (Tables 2 and 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen storage capacity of the CC-Fe and CC-Mn
ce:section.ce:para: With respect to the results shown above, we have performed additional calculations accounting for the interaction of the TM-doped CCs with a higher number H2 molecules. For this purpose, only the most promising systems from the point of view of the H2 binding ability are considered, in particular, the singlet Fe-doped CC (CC–Fe) and the doublet Mn-doped CC (CC–Mn) systems. The B3LYP-GD3/6-311G** optimized CC-Fe and CC-Mn geometries with two, three and four H2 molecules are shown in Fig. 5 , and the calculated Fe–H and Mn–H distances are presented in Table 4 .

Here it is found that, Fe-doped CC is able to bind effectively up to three H2 molecules, all of the Fe–H distances are lower than 2 Å, while the fourth H2 molecule is significantly more distant (nearly 4 Å from Fe atom, see Table 4). Note that, all three H2 molecules bind to the Fe atom in a side-on way, see Fig. 5. In the case of the doublet Mn-doped CC the first two H2 molecules are adsorbed on the Mn atom in the distance lower than 2 Å (similarly as in the case of CC-Fe), while the third one is in the distance of ca. 2.6 Å. The fourth H2 molecule is found to be even more distant than the third one (nearly 4 Å, as in the case of CC-Fe). Our results are in a mutual agreement with the fact that the adsorption/storage ability of the doped GQDs is three H2 molecules on one transition metal center [18,20,31,36].

For completeness, the QTAIM analysis of BCPs for CC-Fe and CC-Mn plus N H2 molecules are compiled in Table S5. The BCP characteristics (e.g. ρ BCP values are around 0.05 bohr−3 and the DI values are around 0.2) presented therein (see Table S5) are in accordance with the calculated Fe–H and Mn–H distances (see Table 4) and point out to the η2-character of the bonds between Fe or Mn and the adsorbed H2 molecules. The exception is the interaction between the Mn atom and the third H2 molecule. The calculated Mn–H distances (ca. 2.6 Å) and the corresponding BCP parameters (ρ BCP value is ca. 0.01 bohr−3 and the DI value is only 0.033) point to a weak interaction, i.e. physisorption. These findings are supported also by the DOS analysis. Partial density of states (PDOS) plots of the Fe (or Mn) and H2 orbitals overlapping in CC-Fe+3H2 (or CC-Mn+3H2) are shown in Fig. 6 . (For a detailed picture of the orbitals overlapping between Fe or Mn and every particular H2 molecule see Fig. S5). One can see from Fig. 6 and Fig. S5 that in the case of the CC-Fe+3H2 system an overlap of Fe and H2 orbitals, indicating a chemical bond, is evidenced for all three H2 molecules, mainly in the region of ca. –15 eV. A similar orbital overlap can be found also in the case of the CC-Mn+3H2 system for Mn and two H2 molecules (i.e. #1H2 and #2H2) in the region of ca. –15 eV. However, there is only a negligible overlap between the orbitals of Mn atom and the third H2 molecule (i.e. #3H2), which again points on an interaction of a physical nature.

The affinity and binding ability of GQDs doped with different transition metals towards H2 molecule have been studied to examine their potential application in the process of hydrogen storage. In the studied systems, the transition metal (TM) dopant atom represents the active H2 adsorbent site. Overall, the most promising results have been obtained for the Fe-doped GQDs, exhibiting the energetically as well as structurally strongest binding to the H2 molecule in their most stable spin state. Further calculations showed that the Fe-doped GQD is able to adsorb three H2 molecules via η2-H2 bonds. In addition, the Cr-, and Mn-doped GQDs may be also used as potential H2 adsorbents, however, their ability to bind H2 is increased when their TM dopants (Cr and Mn) are in the less stable spin states (i.e. singlet and doublet, respectively). Hence, an induced spin state switch in such systems may serve as a method to improve their H2 adsorption ability. Such an action is more easily accomplished in the case of the Mn-doped GQD, due to the lower energy difference between its low-spin and high-spin state when compared to the Cr-doped one. To sum up, the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped GQDs in their low-spin states are prone to H2 binding, but only in the case of the Fe-doped system is the low-spin state energetically preferred. It is found that the Mn-doped GQD (in the doublet spin state) is able to bind two H2 molecules via η2-H2 bonds and the third one via a weak physical interaction. On the contrary, the Co-, Ni-, and Zn-doped GQDs exhibiting only a weak affinity towards H2 molecule when compared to e.g., the Fe-doped analog.

Last but not least, as was already mentioned in the Introduction, the ability to adsorb H2 molecule by a TM-decorated GQDs is even higher in comparison with their TM-doped analogs [35,36]. Decorating TM atom is sterically less hindered than the dopant TM atom and consequently it presents a more accessible adsorption site. Hence, hydrogen adsorption and storage ability of Fe-, Cr-, or Mn-decorated GQDs will be definitely the subject of further studies.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ivan Šalitroš and Ján Pavlík (FCFT STU, Bratislava, Slovakia) are greatly acknowledged for their valuable comments on the manuscript. This work received financial support from Slovak Grant Agencies APVV (contract No. APVV-17-0513, APVV-19-0087 and APVV-20-0213) and VEGA (contracts No. 1/0139/20 and 1/0078/21). We are also grateful to the HPC center at the Slovak University of Technology in Bratislava, which is a part of the Slovak Infrastructure of High Performance Computing (SIVVP project, ITMS code 26230120002, funded by the European region development funds) for the computational time and resources made available. To all financing sources the authors are greatly indebted.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-state effects on the thermal dihydrogen release from solid-state [MH(η2- H2)dppe2]+ (M = Fe, Ru, Os) organometallic complexes for hydrogen storage applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Reiher M, Salomon O, Hess BA (2001) Reparameterization of hybrid functionals based on energy differences of states of different multiplicity. Theor Chem Acc 107:48-55.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Duan Z, Henkelman G (2020) Surface Charge and Electrostatic Spin Crossover Effects in CoN4 Electrocatalysts. ACS Catal 10:12148-12155.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bhandary S, Tomczak JM, Valli A (2021) Designing a mechanically driven spin-crossover molecular switch via organic embedding . Nanoscale Adv 3:4990-4995.
